name: Twilio Voice API
what_it_does: Twilio Voice API enables programmatic inbound and outbound phone calls, interactive voice response (IVR) systems, call recording, transcription, and real-time call control via REST API and TwiML (XML instructions). Used to build anything from notification dialers to full contact center infrastructure.
use_cases: Placing outbound calls from agent workflows (alerts, appointment reminders, survey calls)
Building IVR systems with dynamic TwiML responses that branch on DTMF input
Recording and transcribing calls for later analysis by AI pipelines
Handling inbound calls with automated agent-driven routing and response
Conference call creation and management for multi-party coordination
not_for: SMS messaging (use Twilio Messaging API instead — different product and pricing)
Video calling (use Twilio Video or Zoom API)
High-volume robocalling at consumer scale (legal restrictions apply in most jurisdictions)
Free or near-free calling — per-minute costs accumulate quickly at scale
best_when: An agent needs to place or receive phone calls, record conversations, or build voice-driven interaction flows as part of an automated workflow.
avoid_when: You need video, SMS only, or have extremely tight budget constraints — per-minute voice costs can be significant at scale.

auth.notes: HTTP Basic Auth using Account SID as username and Auth Token (or API Key SID + Secret) as password. API Keys are recommended over Account Auth Token for agent use — they can be rotated without affecting the account. No OAuth or fine-grained scopes; keys provide full account access within the subaccount structure.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Trial account with ~$15 credit; trial numbers can only call verified numbers
pricing.paid_tiers: Outbound calls ~$0.013/min (US); inbound ~$0.0085/min; phone number rental ~$1.15/mo (US local)
pricing.notes: Trial account is useful for testing but restricted to verified numbers. Production use requires upgrading the account. Costs scale linearly with usage — no volume discounts until Enterprise tier. International rates vary significantly by country.

performance.rate_limits: 100 concurrent calls per account by default (increasable); REST API: 100 requests/second per account

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Twilio error responses include a numeric error code, message, and a direct URL to the error documentation page (e.g., https://www.twilio.com/docs/errors/21211). This pattern is one of the best in the industry — agents can log the URL for human debugging. Error codes are stable and well-documented.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No dedicated idempotency key header. Outbound call initiation is not idempotent — duplicate REST calls create duplicate calls. Agents should track Call SIDs after initiation to avoid double-dialing. TwiML responses are stateless and safe to re-serve.
known_agent_gotchas: TwiML must be served from a publicly accessible URL — agents running locally cannot receive inbound calls without a tunnel (ngrok, etc.)
Trial accounts can only call verified phone numbers — test pipelines will silently fail when calling unverified numbers
Call SID must be stored immediately after initiation — there is no reliable way to look up a 'just created' call after the fact without the SID
TwiML <Record> creates a recording but transcription is a separate async step with webhook callback — agents must handle async completion
Phone number provisioning is required before receiving inbound calls and takes a few seconds to propagate
Concurrent call limits (default 100) can be hit by agent loops that dial many numbers simultaneously
Webhook requests from Twilio must be validated via request signature to prevent spoofing

security_score_components.security_notes: API Keys are rotatable and recommended over Account Auth Tokens. No fine-grained scopes — keys provide broad access. Webhook signature verification via HMAC-SHA1 protects against spoofed callbacks. SOC2 and HIPAA compliant.

score_rationale: Twilio Voice API is one of the most agent-friendly telephony APIs available. Documentation is accurate and comprehensive, error messages link to specific docs pages, and the SDK ecosystem is mature across all major languages. The primary agent friction points are the lack of idempotency keys on call initiation, the requirement for a public webhook URL for TwiML, and no scope-based key restrictions. Strong choice when agents need to place or receive calls.
